coredata.dc:title: Silver cluster (Ag6) decorated coronene as non-enzymatic sensor for glucose and H2O2
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling
coredata.dc:creator: Jadoon, Tabish
Mahmood, Tariq
Ayub, Khurshid
coredata.dc:description:
Silver-graphene quantum dots are promising electrochemical sensors due to their unique electronic properties. Herein, we report the comprehensive DFT study to explore the electronic properties of silver cluster (Ag6) decorated coronene as model for silver graphene quantum dots. The current study aims to investigate the sensing ability of silver-coronene complex for non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose & H2O2. The stability of the complexes of analytes with silver decorated coronene is supported by their greater interaction energies (−36.7 to −44.9 kcal mol−1). NBO charge analysis and charge decomposition analysis (CDA) reveal donor-acceptor charge transfer interactions in the complexes. Frontier molecular orbital analysis illustrates that charge is transferred from analytes to silver decorated coronene during excitation from HOMO to LUMO. The Uv–visible results show that
λ max
is red shifted during interactions of analytes with silver decorated coronene. The NCI analysis illustrates the strong non-covalent (M … O) and unusual M … H–O interactions in the complexes. The precedent sensing performance of Ag6-coronene might be attributed to the synergistic effect of both silver clusters and coronene in the composite. The evaluated results validate the excellent sensing ability of silver-graphene quantum dots for the detection of glucose & H2O2. The outcome of the current study and its prospects will open the avenue for the rational development of smart sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver-graphene quantum dots
Glucose & H2O2 detection
Non-enzymatic sensor
Density functional theory
Electronic properties

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometric stability of coronene and Ag6-coronene
Interaction energies & geometric analysis of glucose & H2O2 over silver-coronene composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Side view, (a) glucose@Ag6-coronene, (b) glucose@coronene-Ag6 , (c) H2O2@Ag6-coronene & (d) H2O2@coronene-Ag6.
Frontier molecular orbital representation of glucose and H2O2 complexes.
DOS spectra with HOMO & LUMO visualization of Glucose@Ag6-coronene (top) & H2O2@Ag6-coronene (bottom).

PDOS spectra of (a) Glucose (b) Glucose@Ag6 (c) Glucose@Ag6-coronene respectively. The minor red shifting of
λ max
indicates weak interaction of glucose and H2O2 with coronene. In the silver cluster, the maximum absorbance appears at 394 nm, with excitation energy of 3.14 eV. The crucial transition is from HOMO→LUMO+2. The
λ max
of silver cluster is shifted to a longer wavelength on adsorption of glucose and H2O2. The
λ max
of glucose@Ag6 and H2O2@Ag6 are 405 (3.05 eV) and 402 nm (3.08 eV), respectively. The excitation for glucose@Ag6 is from HOMO→LUMO+2 whereas the excitation associated with
λ max
in H2O2@Ag6 is from HOMO
−
1→LUMO. Surface modification of coronene with the silver cluster is carried out to attain desirable electronic properties of the resultant composite.
Uv–visible spectra of pure Ag6, & H2O2/Glucose@Ag6 calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
Uv–visible spectra of pure coronene, & H2O2/Glucose@coronene calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
Uv–visible spectra of Ag6-coronene & H2O2/Glucose@Ag6-coronene calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.

NCI-RDG 2D scatter map with 3D color-filled RDG isosurface for glucose@Ag6-coronene (top) & H2O2@Ag6-coronene (bottom). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Interaction energies (kcal mol−1) and bond distances (Å) for glucose& H2O2 @Ag6-coronene, @coronene-Ag6, @coronene and @Ag6 substrate calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
Charge transfer analysis for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
UV visible analysis for glucose & H2O2 @Ag6-coronene, @coronene-Ag6, @coronene and @Ag6 substrate calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
Charge Decomposition Analysis (CDA) results calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.

head.ce:title: Silver cluster (Ag6) decorated coronene as non-enzymatic sensor for glucose and H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Silver-graphene quantum dots are promising electrochemical sensors due to their unique electronic properties. Herein, we report the comprehensive DFT study to explore the electronic properties of silver cluster (Ag6) decorated coronene as model for silver graphene quantum dots. The current study aims to investigate the sensing ability of silver-coronene complex for non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose & H2O2. The stability of the complexes of analytes with silver decorated coronene is supported by their greater interaction energies (−36.7 to −44.9 kcal mol−1). NBO charge analysis and charge decomposition analysis (CDA) reveal donor-acceptor charge transfer interactions in the complexes. Frontier molecular orbital analysis illustrates that charge is transferred from analytes to silver decorated coronene during excitation from HOMO to LUMO. The Uv–visible results show that
λ max
is red shifted during interactions of analytes with silver decorated coronene. The NCI analysis illustrates the strong non-covalent (M … O) and unusual M … H–O interactions in the complexes. The precedent sensing performance of Ag6-coronene might be attributed to the synergistic effect of both silver clusters and coronene in the composite. The evaluated results validate the excellent sensing ability of silver-graphene quantum dots for the detection of glucose & H2O2. The outcome of the current study and its prospects will open the avenue for the rational development of smart sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is one of the widespread metabolic diseases that is caused by hyperglycemia, an elevated glucose level in the blood. Diabetes has become one of the increasing and highly deliberating diseases to public health. If the current trend persists, it will be the 7th leading cause of death in 2030. Precautionary measures to balance the blood glucose level in a diabetic patient include changing lifestyle, dietary pattern, and hyperglycemic drugs [1–5]. Frequent measurement of blood glucose level is the only possible way to understand the hypo and hyperglycemic conditions to prevent the patient from further complications such as stroke, heart attack, renal failure, and blindness [1]. The patients with high level diabetes or having renal glycosuria can be identified by the glucose level in their urine.

H2O2, a valuable biological molecule with oxidizing characteristic, is involved in numerous biological reactions and it is used in the synthesis of many organic compounds. H2O2 is also a byproduct of various oxidases enzymes such as H2O2 oxidase, lactate oxidase. H2O2 is produced as a byproduct during the oxidation of glucose by an enzyme glucose oxidase [6].

For the quantitative measurement of glucose and H2O2 level, enzymatic sensors are commonly used [7]. The detection principle of enzymatic sensors is principally based on either indirectly measuring the byproducts of reaction such as hydrogen peroxide or directly measuring the transfer of an electron from the enzyme to an electrode. The serious drawbacks linked with these enzymatic sensors, which ultimately affect the precision of the glucose measurements, are the dependency of these sensors on pH, humidity, temperature, and ascorbic acid etc. For these reasons, there is a recent need to develop non-enzymatic sensors for the selective and accurate measurement of glucose and H2O2 concentration [8–13].

Transition metal nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots have been frequently used for the detection of various analytes such as NH3, H2S, TNT, metal ions, biological small molecules, DNA, H2O2, and glucose [14–21]. Silver metal nanoparticles based biosensors have been developed for the sensing of glucose and H2O2 due to their high electrical conductivity [22]. The high cost of silver metal tends to limit its practical applications in sensors.

The biosensors and chemical sensor based on 2D material have been frequently used for sensing applications [23–32] Moreover, the composites of transition metals with low cost carbon-based substrate such as graphene oxide, graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNT), and graphene quantum dots are widely used for sensing purpose [33–35]. It has been recognized that the silver-graphene composites contribute significantly towards the detection of various analytes due to their superior electronic properties [36–45]. Graphene metal hybrid nanomaterials find their broad applications for the electrochemical sensing of glucose as well as for the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 [36,46–50]. These nanostructured materials as peroxidase mimetics show unparalleled advantages in terms of low cost and stability over natural enzymes [51–54].

Literature reveals that although silver nanoparticles based graphene composites have been experimentally investigated for non-enzymatic electrochemical sensing of glucose and H2O2 but comprehensive theoretical study is not yet reported [36,46,47]. It is the need of the day to get complete insights about the sensing mechanism of silver-graphene hybrid composites for the development of smart sensors. Earlier, we performed a comprehensive theoretical study about the electronic properties of 2–10 atoms silver clusters decorated graphene nanoflakes [55]. The improved electronic properties of silver cluster decorated graphene systems encourage us to investigate their sensing properties as well. The literature contains only a handful reports where such composite structures are studied theoretically for interaction with analytes. More particularly, graphene-metal clusters are very scarce. However, theoretical studies on such or similar models are easily available in the literature. For example, M13 cluster (M = Fe, Ni and Cu) on coronene surface are studied to get insight about their electronic and chemical properties. In another report, Pdn clusters are adsorbed on coronene to model Pd-graphene composites [56–58]. Similarly, interaction of oligothiophene with small graphene analogues is studied as model for graphene-polythiophene composites [59].

We herein, present a first detailed theoretical study about one of the coplanar silver cluster (Ag6) decorated graphene quantum dots from our previous work, as a smart sensor for the non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose and H2O2.

Density functional theory (DFT) simulations are carried out with Gaussian 09 whereas Gauss View 5.0 program package is used for visualization of results. Frequency calculations are performed to confirm that optimized structures are true minima. All quantum chemical calculations are performed at M06-2X/LANL2DZ level of theory. M06-2X is the best functional for accurate determination of interaction energies of transition metals based system [60]. M06-2X is a highly parameterized Minnesota functional that uses a much cleaner functional form for both the exchange and the correlation. M06-2X shows the best performance for noncovalent interactions as it includes (on average) 94% of the empirically required dispersion-like interactions. In our previous study we adopted two approaches (using two different basis sets) to compute interaction energies of silver-graphene system at low computational cost compared with high level ab-initio method. In first approach we used mixed basis set i.e. LANL2DZ basis set for silver atoms and 6-31G(d,p) for all other atoms in the system for the computation of interaction energies. As 6-31G(d,p) contains polarization function but the results obtained by using mixed basis set approach are not much convincing. In second approach we used single ECPs (LANL2DZ) for all atoms in the system. The results of interaction energies obtained with LANL2DZ basis set were in good agreement with reported high level methods. We performed benchmark calculations (using various DFT functional) for the selection of an appropriate functional to accurately describe interaction energies of silver-graphene system. The value of interaction energy (for Ag4/coronene) obtained with M06-2X/LANL2DZ was in fair agreement with reported value at MP2/ANO-RCC-VDZ in the literature [61]. The selection of M06-2X/LANL2DZ method is thus based on its superior performance for interaction energies calculations over other functionals [62]. Therefore, in this study, we kept the same level of theory for further calculations.

The interaction energies were calculated for several possible orientations for the adsorption of analytes over substrate. Out of all investigated orientations the one with highest interaction energies (in each case) are reported in the manuscript in order to avoid any complexity, the interaction energies of rest of the configurations are given in the supporting information.

Interaction energies (
E
i n t
) of analytes (H2O2 & glucose) on pure coronene, pure silver cluster and silver-coronene complex are calculated with equations (1)–(3), respectively.
(1)
E int
=
E
coronene … analyte
−
(
E Coronene
+
E analyte
)
(2)
E int
=
E
A
g 6
… analyte
−
(
E
A
g 6
+
E analyte
)
(3)
E int
=
E
(
Coronene − A
g 6
)
… analyte
−
(
E
Coronene − A
g 6
+
E analyte
)

To get complete insights about the electronic properties, the lowest energy structures are further investigated through NBO charge analysis & frontier molecular orbital analysis. NBO charge analysis gives an idea about the amount of charge transfer during interactions. The excited state calculations are carried out with time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) which describes all possible excitations from occupied to virtual orbitals. The HOMO (an electron donor) and the LUMO (an electron acceptor) are very important in quantum chemistry for studying the interactions. The small LUMO and HOMO energy gap suggests the high reactivity of the system. Total density of states (TDOS) analysis is carried out by the Gauss sum program to understand the number of available energy states in the complexes. The orbital contribution to PDOS, Non-covalent interactions index (NCI) & charge decomposition analysis (CDA) are performed by Multiwfn 3.6 software package to understand the nature of interactions between various fragments of the complexes of glucose and H2O2.

Results of frequency calculations reveal that all the optimized structures are true minima with no imaginary frequency. The most stable structures for each orientation are used for various properties calculations. The interaction energies and geometries for the complexes of analytes with pure silver cluster and coronene are given in the supporting information (Fig. SS1 and Fig. SS2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometric stability of coronene and Ag6-coronene
ce:section.ce:para:
The geometric stability and durability of the prime material is guaranteed with cohesive energy of formation [30–32,63]. Herein, the cohesive formation energy of the base substrate coronene, is calculated with the help of equation given below;
(4)
E Coh
=
1
n + m
[
E Coronene
− n
E C
− m
E H
]
E
C o r o n e n e
is the energy of pristine coronene consisting of
n + m
atoms where n and m denote the number of carbon and hydrogen atoms, respectively.
E C
and
E H
are the energies of individual carbon and hydrogen atoms, respectively. The computationally calculated cohesive formation energy of coronene with the help of above equation is
–
6.75 eV/atom. The negative value of cohesive formation energy assures the stability of the base substrate, coronene. Similarly, the cohesive energy of Ag6-coronene is also calculated. The cohesive energy for this cluster is −5.97 eV/atom. The cohesive energy of the cluster is a bit lower than pure coronene but the value is high enough to guarantee the stability of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction energies & geometric analysis of glucose & H2O2 over silver-coronene composites
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction distance of silver cluster from coronene is quite large in Ag6-coronene but, at the same time, interaction energy for composite formation is quite reasonable. The composite formed has binding energy of −23.3 kcal mol−1, despite the large interaction distance of 3.38 Å. The reasonably high interaction energy indicates that the silver-coronene composite formed is quite stable thermodynamically (the adsorption energy is in chemisorption range). The selection of coplanar Ag6 cluster is because it would give us a model closer to reality where many silver atoms (in the cluster) can interact with the analytes.

The interaction mechanism of analytes (glucose & H2O2) with two configurations of silver-coronene composites is investigated here to check the most suitable binding. In configuration A (analyte@Ag6-coronene), the analyte is directly present over the silver cluster side of silver-coronene composite whereas in configuration B (analyte@coronene-Ag6), analyte is directly present over the coronene side of silver-coronene composite (Fig. 1 ).

The interaction energies for H2O2@Ag6-coronene & glucose@Ag6-coronene complexes are −44.9 and −39.9 kcal mol−1, respectively. In complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-coronene (configuration A), the silver atom prefers to bind with the oxygen atom of analytes. In both complexes the minimum distance between analyte and composite (Ag… O) is same (i.e. 2.67 Å). The interaction energy of H2O2@Ag6-coronene is greater than glucose@Ag6-coronene because of steric repulsions in glucose ring at a shorter distance beside metal-oxygen electrostatic interactions.
The interaction mechanism of analytes with silver-coronene composite (configuration B) reveals that the carbon atom of the composite preferably binds with the hydrogen atom of analytes.

The interaction energies for H2O2@coronene-Ag6 & glucose@coronene-Ag6 complexes are −40.2 & −36.7 kcal mol−1, respectively. The C⋯H distances of 2.54 & 2.49 Å are observed for the adsorption of H2O2 & glucose over configuration B of silver-coronene composites, respectively. Although, the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with configuration A & B show a similar trend in interaction energies, the complexes of these analytes with A are relatively more stable than B. The difference in interaction energies for analytes over Ag6-coronene configuration A and B is the strength of electrostatic and dispersive interactions. The electronegative oxygen atom of analyte directly interacts with Ag metal atom in configuration A. The highly electronegative oxygen atom provides an efficient way for the strong electrostatic interactions with highly electropositive metal (silver) atom. In configuration B where analyte directly interacts with coronene surface of the silver-coronene composite the C⋯H dispersive interactions are dominating. The higher interaction energies for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with Ag6-coronene (configuration A) than coronene-Ag6 (configuration B) are due to strong electrostatic forces in configuration A than weak dispersive forces in configuration B.

The interaction energy is one of the important stability indicators and results of interaction energies clearly indicate that complexes of H2O2 are more stable than the corresponding complexes of glucose with silver-coronene composites (configuration A & B), pure silver cluster and coronene, respectively. The greater stability of the complexes of these analytes with silver-coronene is due to the synergic effect of silver cluster and coronene in the composite. The stability of the complexes follows the following order i.e. H2O2@Ag6-coronene>H2O2@coronene-Ag6>glucose@Ag6-coronene>glucose@coronene-Ag6 > H2O2@Ag6 > glucose@Ag6>H2O2@coronene > glucose@coronene.

The electronic and structural properties of interacting species change due to charge transfer interaction between two interacting species (see Table 1 ). Natural bond orbital (NBO) charge analysis is performed for glucose and H2O2 over isolated co-planer Ag6, isolated coronene and Ag6-coronene composites and the results of charge analysis are given in Table 2 . The NBO charge analysis of glucose@Ag6 complex indicates that −0.115
e −
charge is transferred from glucose to the silver cluster. Silver cluster behaves like a good electric charge acceptor and binds to the electron rich oxygen atom of glucose through highly stable O...Ag bonds. The charge is transferred from the lone pair of oxygen atom of glucose to the empty d-orbitals of silver cluster. The amount of charge transfer in glucose@coronene complex is −0.006
e −
, which is less than charge transfer in glucose@Ag6 complex. The less amount of charge transfer in glucose@coronene could probably be due to low electrophilic nature of coronene besides delocalization of charge in the coronene ring.

In complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-coronene composites, the amount of charge transfer is greater than the complexes of these analytes with silver cluster and coronene alone. Transfer of electronic cloud from glucose and H2O2 to silver-coronene composites shows that these substrates are good sensing platforms for the detection of these analytes. The charges transferred in glucose@Ag6-coronene (configuration A) and glucose@coronene-Ag6 (configuration B) are 0.241
e −
and 0.107
e −
, respectively. The greater charge transfer in configuration A than B results from stable O⋯ Ag interactions in A compared to weak H⋯C interactions in B. The amount of charge transfer in H2O2@ Ag6-coronene and H2O2@coronene-Ag6 complexes are 0.033
e −
and 0.007
e −
, respectively.

Although there is greater NBO charge transfer in the complexes of glucose than H2O2, the trend of charge transfer is quite similar in the complexes of both analytes. To gain deep insight into the factors responsible for weak charge transfer in H2O2 complexes (than glucose complexes), we have performed charge decomposition analysis (vide infra). The overall weak charge transfer in the complexes of H2O2 than the complexes of glucose is due to the greater value of repulsive polarization in H2O2 (when compared with glucose). Moreover, the greater number of oxygen atoms of glucose interact strongly with silver atoms in the cluster (than H2O2) which result in more Ag⋯O charge transfer electrostatic interactions in the former. NBO charge transfer follows the following order i.e. analyte@silver-coronene > analyte@Ag6 > analyte@coronene.

The interaction mechanism of analytes over the surfaces of a silver cluster (Ag6), coronene and Ag6-coronene composites are described through the theory of frontier molecular orbital. The change in energy of HOMO and LUMO during interactions is a valuable indicator for sensing mechanism. It is easier to accept and donate electrons for material with lower E LUMO and higher E HOMO, respectively.

The conductivity of the material is related to the H-L gap, and materials with a smaller H-L gap show higher conductivity and vice versa. The results of the FMO analysis for the complexes of analytes with silver cluster, coronene, and silver-coronene composites are summarized in Fig. 2 . The HOMO and LUMO energies of isolated coronene are
−
6.79 and
−
0.9 eV, respectively with observed H-L gap of 5.89 eV. The adsorption of H2O2 and glucose over coronene results in similar decrease in the H-L gap (0.01 eV). Small decrease in the H-L gap arises due to poor orbital overlapping between analytes and coronene. In the case of a pure silver cluster (Ag6), the H-L gap of 4.02 eV is observed, which decreases to 4.00 & 3.92 eV on interaction with H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The prominent decrease in the H-L gap in the case of glucose@Ag6 (0.08 eV) than H2O2@Ag6 (0.02 eV) results from the strong overlapping between the orbitals of Ag-atom with O-atom of glucose.

The silver cluster decorated coronene composites are valuable adsorption materials for the sensing of glucose and H2O2. The H-L gap of 3.83 eV is observed in the silver-coronene composite. For H2O2@Ag6-coronene and H2O2@coronene-Ag6 complexes, the H-L gaps are increased to 3.87 & 3.84 eV, respectively. The change in the H-L gap is greater for H2O2@Ag6-coronene (0.03 eV) than H2O2@coronene-Ag6 (0.01 eV). As chemical sensors work based on the change of electrical conductivity or resistivity, and it is expected that the resistivity is increased by increasing the H-L gap and vice versa. The remarkable change in the H-L gap is noticed for the complexes of glucose than the complexes of H2O2 with silver-coronene composites.

The H-L gaps of glucose@Ag6-coronene and glucose@coronene-Ag6 complexes are 3.76 & 3.84 eV, respectively. The change in the H-L gap for glucose@Ag6-coronene (0.07 eV), is greater than the H-L gap in a glucose@coronene-Ag6 complex (0.01 eV). The prominent decrease in the H-L gap in complexes of both analytes with configuration A indicates their high sensitivity. The negligible change in the H-L gap in complexes of both analytes in configuration B indicates that the conductivity of silver-coronene composite will not change notably during complexation with these analytes. The results of the current finding suggest that silver-coronene composites are better sensing materials for the detection of glucose than H2O2.

DOS analysis gives us the number of available energy states as a function of energy per unit volume. The total density of states (TDOS) analysis is performed to get an idea about the electronic perturbations in complexes due to the adsorption of analytes. The intensity of energy levels in DOS spectra directly express the degree of degeneracy in the system.
Changes that occur in the region of HOMO and LUMO give fruitful information about the sensing abilities of the conducting materials.

DOS spectra with HOMO-LUMO visualization for the complexes of H2O2 and glucose with pure Ag6, pure coronene & Ag6-coronene composites (configuration A & B) are given in Fig. 3 (supporting information Fig. SS4). In isolated coronene, both HOMO and LUMO orbitals are purely located on coronene at.

−
6.79 and
−
0.90 eV, respectively. For glucose@coronene & H2O2@coronene, H-L gaps decrease to the same extent (1%). In glucose@coronene complex, HOMO & LUMO reside on coronene component but in H2O2@coronene complex HOMO is partially located over both coronene and H2O2 components whereas LUMO entirely on coronene. The insignificant drop in the H-L gap in the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with coronene advocate poor sensitivity of coronene for these analytes. In glucose@Ag6 complex, H-L gap decreases by 10% to that of pure silver cluster. The HOMO and LUMO orbitals are located on the both components silver cluster and glucose. The greater sensitivity of glucose@Ag6 complex than H2O2@Ag6 complex is apparent from greater number of peaks in HOMO and LUMO side of the spectra.

Silver-coronene composites are the emerging semiconducting materials which act as better sensing platform than coronene and silver cluster. TDOS calculations are also performed for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-coronene (configuration A & B). For the glucose@Ag6-coronene complex, H-L gap decrease by 7% to that of silver-coronene composite. The HOMO orbitals are entirely located over the silver cluster component whereas LUMO orbitals on the coronene component of the complex. The generation of new HOMO and LUMO orbitals in DOS spectra certainly affirm the electronic perturbation in the complex. H-L gap decreases by
−
4% when H2O2 interact with Ag6-coronene. HOMO is mainly located over the silver cluster with small density on analyte while LUMO is located purely on the coronene component in the complex. The sensing ability of the complex is noticeable from the generation of new peaks in the spectra. The complexes of analytes with silver-coronene composite (configuration B) show lesser sensing capacity than configuration A. The decrease in H-L gap of
−
1% is noticed for glucose@coronene-Ag6 and H2O2@coronene-Ag6 complexes. In these complexes, the variation of energy of states are like that of silver-coronene composite. The HOMO orbitals are entirely located on the silver cluster whereas LUMO over coronene component in these complexes.

PDOS spectral analysis is carried out to show the individual orbital contribution to the total density of states (TDOS) [51]. Fig. 4 shows the PDOS of p orbital of the oxygen atom of the glucose once it is in the glucose molecule, attached to the Ag6 and Ag6-coronene surfaces. The PDOS spectra reveal the sole contribution of p orbitals of oxygen atoms in occupied frontier molecular orbitals of glucose@Ag6 and glucose@Ag6-coronene while d-states of silver metal do not show any contribution. The sole contribution of oxygen p orbital in HOMO advocates the transference of electronic cloud from p orbital of oxygen atom of glucose to the d orbital of silver metal in these complexes. Moreover, the peaks associated with p orbital of oxygen appeared close to the Fermi level in HOMO side of spectra beside origination of new energy states clarify the higher sensitivity and greater charge transfer in glucose@Ag6 and glucose@Ag6-coronene complexes.

Excited state calculations are carried out with TD-DFT theory for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver cluster, coronene and silver-coronene composites (Table 3 ). The UV–visible spectra of these complexes are given in Figs. 5–7
. The observed
λ max
for coronene appeared at 262 nm with excitation energy of 4.71 eV. The maximum absorbance in coronene is related to the dominant transition from HOMO
−
1→LUMO (π→π∗). Bathochromic shift is observed for the complexes of coronene with glucose and H2O2. The
λ max
are slightly red shifted to 265 and 263 nm for glucose@coronene and H2O2@coronene, respectively.

The silver-coronene composite shows
λ max
of 435 nm and excitation energy of 2.84 eV related to dominant HOMO
−
1→LUMO+4 transition. The observed maximum absorbance of the silver-coronene composite is in reasonable agreement with the reported value of 425 nm for silver-graphene nanocomposites [46]. The results of excited state calculations reveal that silver-coronene composite is a better sensing platform for glucose and H2O2 compared to coronene and silver cluster alone. The change in absorption spectrum on going from Ag6 to Ag6@coronene is because for Ag6, HOMO-LUMO gap is high (4.02 eV) which decreases upon composite formation with coronene (3.83 eV). Distribution of densities in frontier molecular orbitals indicates that the decrease in the energy gap is due to better overlap between frontier orbitals. The HOMO in the composite is located on the metal center whereas the LUMO is present on the coronene part. The electronic excitation from HOMO to LUMO is a shift of density from the Ag6 part to the coronene part. This shift might be responsible for decrease in excitation energies which ultimately affect the absorption spectra.

The observed
λ max
in glucose@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration A) is 441 nm with excitation energy of 2.81 eV. The wavelength is red-shifted while the crucial transition is HOMO→LUMO+4 in the complex. For the H2O2@Ag6-coronene complex, maximum absorbance is noticed at 429 nm. The excitation energy required for the crucial transition from HOMO
−
1→LUMO+4 is 2.88 eV. Interaction of glucose with Ag6-coronene complex causes red shifting of absorption spectrum whereas H2O2 causes blue shift, although the shift is the same for both. The results of absorption spectrum cannot be correlated with the results from other ground state properties because absorption spectrum is the property of excited states. Involvement of different excitation level in different species leads to the trends which are different from those of other ground state properties. Here for the complexes of analytes with silver-coronene composites (configuration B), little hypsochromic shift is noticed. The wavelength of maximum absorbance (
λ max
) at 432 nm (2.80 eV) for glucose@coronene-Ag6 involves excitation of electron from HOMO→LUMO+3. Quite similar blue shifting of wavelength is also observed in the H2O2@coronene-Ag6 complex. The
λ max
of 433 nm (2.85 eV) involves dominant HOMO
−
1→LUMO+3 transition.

The little hypsochromic shift in complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-coronene composite (configuration B) shows that resistivity of conductivity increases slightly during complexation. Ag6-coronene is quite interesting sensor because, upon interaction with analytes, not only the electronic structure of the sensor is changed but also the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Hence, these sensors can work as an electrochemical as well as chemo sensor. The outcome of current FMO analysis suggests that silver-coronene composites show better sensing performance than pure silver cluster and coronene. Furthermore, the silver-coronene composites have good sensing ability for glucose than H2O2.

NCI is a robust technique which is based on density and reduced density gradient (RDG). NCI gives insight about nature of non-covalent interactions such as van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonds, and steric clashes in complexes. The RDG is calculated by taking the first derivative of electron density ρ(r).
R D G =
1
2
(
3
π 2
)
1 / 3
| ∇ ρ
( r )
|
ρ
( r )
4 / 3
Where ρ and ∇ρ are referred to as electronic density and electronic density gradient, respectively. RDG has a very large positive value far away from the molecule as electron density exponentially decreases to zero there. The RDG has very small values, close to zero in the region of covalent and non-covalent interactions. The types of interactions are determined by considering the three eigenvalues (λi) of Hessian (λ1≤ λ2≤ λ3). All the eigenvalues are negative at nuclei where electron density is maximum. Two of the eigenvalues are negative while third one is positive for covalent interactions. To distinguish between bonded and non-bonded interactions λ2(ρ) is used.

For bonded interactions the eigenvalue λ2<0 and for non-bonded repulsive interactions λ2>0. The strength of non-covalent interactions is directly related to the density. NCI-RDG 2D scatter map and 3D color-filled RDG isosurface for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-coronene composite (configuration A) is given in Fig. 8 (see supporting information Fig.SS6-SS9 for rest of the complexes).

NCI results reveal that for the complexes of analytes with pure silver cluster and silver-coronene (configuration A), low density peaks (λ2ρ) appear at
−
0.03 to
−
0.02 a. u. These peaks are associated with M … O electrostatic interactions. The presence of electrostatic interactions is also depicted from higher interaction energies of these complexes. Peaks which appear in NCI plot of these complexes at
−
0.01 and 0.01 to 0.02 a. u. are related to van der Waals interactions and steric repulsions, respectively. Moreover, blue-green disc-like NCI isosurface is observed between the silver cluster and analytes in these complexes which also indicates weak van der Waals interactions in the complexes. In complexes of analytes with coronene and silver-coronene (configuration B), peaks which appear at λ2ρ =
−
0.03 to
−
0.02 are related with H-bonding and uncommon M … H–O interactions, respectively. Peaks originates at λ2ρ = −0.01 and λ2ρ = 0.02 a. u. indicate the van der Waals interactions and steric clashes in these complexes. The blue-green disc-like NCI isosurface between analytes and coronene surface indicates weak van der Waals (C⋯H) dispersion interactions.

CDA analysis is carried out to investigate the charge transfer between various fragments pair in the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with pure silver cluster, coronene, and silver-coronene composites (Table 3). Herein, CDA results are presented to focus on the relative amount of forward donation and repulsive polarization of charges in the complexes.

The relative donation of charges in glucose-Ag6 (0.000
e −
), Ag6-coronene (0.035
e −
) fragments pair in glucose@coronene-Ag6 complex and H2O2–Ag6 (0.039
e −
) fragment pair in H2O2–Ag6-coronene complex suggest that charge transfer via donation dominate over backdonation. In rest of the fragment’s pairs of complexes the values of relative donation indicate that charge transfer trough backdonation is dominating over donation. The repulsion of charges is an important indicator of charge density between donor and acceptor fragments. The values of repulsive polarization in H2O2-coronene (H2O2@coronene), glucose-Ag6 (glucose@coronene-Ag6), H2O2–Ag6 (H2O2–Ag6-coronene) and H2O2–Ag6 (H2O2@coronene-Ag6 fragments pairs are 0.119
e −
, 0.001
e −
, 0.012
e −
and 0.113
e −
, respectively. These values of repulsion indicate that charge density accumulates in the interaction region between these fragment’s pairs. In all other fragment’s pairs of the complexes the charge density does not concentrate rather it is moving away from the interaction region (Table 4 ).

Silver graphene quantum dots are promising sensors for the investigation of one of the widespread metabolic disease “Diabetes mellitus” due to their unique electronic properties. In conclusion, we present the greater sensing performance of silver graphene composite towards glucose & H2O2 than silver cluster and graphene alone is due to the synergistic effect of both components in the composite. Silver cluster (Ag6) decorated coronene is taken as the simple model for silver graphene quantum dots. The complexes of H2O2 with silver coated coronene are more stable than complexes of glucose. The stability of the complexes of analytes with silver coated coronene is supported by their greater interaction energies (−36.7 to −44.9 kcal mol−1). The interaction of glucose and H2O2 with silver cluster and coronene in the composite is mainly because of M … O & unusual M … H–O hydrogen bond. Significant reduction in H-L gap, red shifting of
λ max
, charge transfer between various fragments pair, strong non-covalent (H-bond) interactions and generation of new peaks along with their intensities in DOS spectra in the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver-complexes illustrate their good sensitivity. The silver coated coronene has good sensing ability for glucose than H2O2. Ag6-coronene is quite interesting sensor because, upon interaction with analytes, not only the electronic structure of the sensor is changed but also the Uv–vis absorption spectra are also changed. Therefore, these sensors can work in dual mode i.e., electrochemical and chemo sensor. The current study will provide new avenues towards further exploration of silver cluster graphene hybrid composites for the development of smart sensors in various applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial and technical support from COMSATS University, Abbottabad Campus and Higher Education Commission of Pakistan (Grant # 1899, 2469, 2981).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Table S1– S7 shows the interaction energies of all possible configurations. Fig. SS3 shows HOMO & LUMO for the complexes of glucose and H2O2 with silver cluster, coronene, and silver-coronene composite. Fig. SS4 & SS5 shows DOS & UV–visible spectra for the complexes, respectively. Fig. SS6-SS9 show the NCI plot for the complexes.
Application Application

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmgm.2020.107824.
